posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: This was my first time with this producer and I was really impressed. Striking complexity for this level. Lots of minerality, juicy white fruits, a little flint, even some tropical notes—all nicely balanced. Not huge density of material and, perhaps owing to the year, a touch unstructured, but still very impressive and very quaffable, including working well with food. Glad I have a couple more.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Thanks for the check in. The hope from some is that the '18s will add structure over time. I think they already have to some degree in the past 2 years.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Thanks Joshua.

I had the 2018 Anne Boisson Bourgogne Aligote yesterday. Incredibly good. Rich and unctuous and cut by lemon and minerals.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: This his best Bourgogne (he makes a few of them), and comes from a seriously well situated piece of land too.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: My understanding is that Pierre’s and Anne’s wines are all made together, in the same way, that it’s one operation, but with only the labels being different. Do you or @Ian_Dorin know if that’s correct?

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
That’s correct. What I had heard about the 2 different labels was that because of French inheritance law, the properties had to be split among the siblings since they both wanted claim.

But yes, it’s one house, and one style. The siblings make the decisions together.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Thanks, Ian–interesting arrangement. I can’t off the top of my head think of similar ones in Burgundy, but I’m not all that knowledgeable about these things.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Usually they break off into 2 Domaines, and don’t normally work together

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked:
Ian_Dorin:

That’s correct. What I had heard about the 2 different labels was that because of French inheritance law, the properties had to be split among the siblings since they both wanted claim.

But yes, it’s one house, and one style. The siblings make the decisions together.

Not sure that is quite true anymore. As @William_Kelley wrote last year:

On this visit, Boisson père explained that in fact Pierre and Anne operate separately though cooperatively (Pierre taking care of all the tractor work in the vineyards) and vinify in their own way—though the two labels’ styles are undeniably more similar than different.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Interesting! I hope to visit there in April when I go to Burgundy.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I reported what I was told… but it must be said, the narrative isn’t always consistent. One thing you learn in Burgundy is that, “everything is the same” can mean that everything is literally the same, or that e.g. the wines are all Chardonnay and all fermented in barrel but other than that handled quite differently, or simply that the wines are made with the same general aesthetic ideal in mind.

What I can say is that while the sites are different the two ranges, always presented intermingled together, do taste like one quite consistent range. And what I do know is that, according to Bernard last year, Pierre does all the tractor work.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Thanks, William,

One way or another, they do seem to be making some very nice whites.

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked: I had this recently (along with the Monthelie & Auxey 1er rouge). I liked the blanc. Not too weighty at the moment but very clean and complex. I should’ve gone back to it later, but others were enjoying it. Nice to have in the rotation along with Fichet and Javillier.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: I’ve been reading about Pierre and Anne for a while now and haven’t yet tasted the wines. The ones mentioned here are in the $60-$70 range in the US. I’m out of touch … is this what a good Bourgogne Blanc goes for in 2023? The ‘basic’ Bourgogne Blanc from the Boissons is in the $30s.

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked:
R_Frankel:

The ones mentioned here are in the $60-$70 range in the US. I’m out of touch … is this what a good Bourgogne Blanc goes for in 2023?

Don’t look at Coche or Lamy Caillat then! Even outside of that, yes, a lot of producers’ BBs have reached that level (quick search of PYCM, d’Angerville). Others are not quite there (Carillon, Bernard Moreau) but are ticking up.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: They are in that price range when you are talking about 100 year old vines. It’s also close to where all the better vineyards of Meursault is too.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: FWIW, these were right around $50, but they are somewhat less recent purchases.And I agree with @Ian_Dorin–very well worth it–just as I find Roulot’s or even Ante’s back when they were in the $60-70 range.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Roulot is now $100+ and Ente I can’t even believe the price!!!

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked: There was a recent sale with a very sharp pricing.
